series_items.subject: [PATCH 1/8] mm: memcontrol: propagate NMI slab stats to memcg vmstats
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series addresses an inconsistency in the propagation of NMI slab statistics to memcg-level vmstats. The current implementation, flush_nmi_stats(), only drains per-node NMI slab atomics into lruvec_stats but not into memcg-level vmstats. This is fixed by propagating the NMI slab stats to both per-node lruvec_stats and memcg-level vmstats.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 2/8] mm: percpu: charge obj_exts allocation with __GFP_ACCOUNT
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series introduces per-memcg-per-node kmem accounting for the percpu allocator, which is a preparatory step for upcoming changes. The first patch in the series modifies the obj_exts allocation to charge metadata and payload separately by passing __GFP_ACCOUNT to the allocation function.

review_comments.author: Shakeel Butt
review_comments.summary: Raised questions about charging obj_exts array to a single memcg, highlighting potential overcharging and undercharging of nodes. Asked for clarification on alloc_percpu() allocation behavior and the impact of not charging obj_exts.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Mon, May 11, 2026 at 10:20:37PM +0200, Alexandre Ghiti wrote:
> This is a preparatory patch for upcoming per-memcg-per-node kmem
> accounting.
> 
> pcpu allocations are always fully charged at once using
> pcpu_obj_full_size(), which returns the size of the pcpu "metadata" +
> pcpu "payload". But metadata and payload may not be allocated on the
> same numa node, so charge the metadata independently from the payload.
> 
> Do this by explicitly passing __GFP_ACCOUNT to the obj_exts allocation
> and remove its accounting in pcpu_memcg_pre_alloc_hook().

Will all the entries in obj_exts array be for the same memcg? If not then why we
are charging the whole array to the one which happen to allocate the array?

Sorry I don't know the details of percpu allocator, so asking some dumb
questions:

1. Does the alloc_percpu() (& similar functions) allocate the underlying on a
   single node or does it allocate memory for each cpu on their local node?
   For slub, it is on the same node, so the situation is easier to handle.

2. On a typical system how much memory is consumed by obj_exts for the percpu
   allocator chunks? I am wondering if we don't charge it, how much will we
   loose?

3. What would be side effect on assuming that obj_exts is on the same node as
   the given chunk?

review_comments.author: Alexandre Ghiti
review_comments.summary: Acknowledged the oversight in charging metadata and payload separately, estimated the size of obj_exts to be around 64kB. Recognized that dynamically allocated chunk fields are not accounted for, which represents an additional 2.3kB.
review_comments.raw_body:
Hi Shakeel,

On 5/21/26 19:25, Shakeel Butt wrote:
> On Mon, May 11, 2026 at 10:20:37PM +0200, Alexandre Ghiti wrote:
>> This is a preparatory patch for upcoming per-memcg-per-node kmem
>> accounting.
>>
>> pcpu allocations are always fully charged at once using
>> pcpu_obj_full_size(), which returns the size of the pcpu "metadata" +
>> pcpu "payload". But metadata and payload may not be allocated on the
>> same numa node, so charge the metadata independently from the payload.
>>
>> Do this by explicitly passing __GFP_ACCOUNT to the obj_exts allocation
>> and remove its accounting in pcpu_memcg_pre_alloc_hook().
> Will all the entries in obj_exts array be for the same memcg? If not then why we
> are charging the whole array to the one which happen to allocate the array?


Hmm, I overlooked the amount allocated, so that's my mistake: the 
chunk-allocating-memcg will be charged for all the metadata, although 
before the charge was distributed. And according to Claude, the metadata 
would represent 64kB, so not negligible.


>
> Sorry I don't know the details of percpu allocator, so asking some dumb
> questions:
>
> 1. Does the alloc_percpu() (& similar functions) allocate the underlying on a
>     single node or does it allocate memory for each cpu on their local node?
>     For slub, it is on the same node, so the situation is easier to handle.


To me, chunk metadata and actual pages are allocated differently:

- pcpu_alloc_pages() tries to allocate the pages on the cpu local node 
https://elixir.bootlin.com/linux/v7.0.9/source/mm/percpu-vm.c#L95. But 
to me no guarantee it won't fallback to any other node. And I don't 
think that __GFP_THISNODE would be a good idea here.

- pcpu_alloc_chunk() uses kmalloc or vmalloc depending on the size, so 
not attached to specific node, that's why I wanted GFP_ACCOUNT to do the 
job for us in the first place.


>
> 2. On a typical system how much memory is consumed by obj_exts for the percpu
>     allocator chunks? I am wondering if we don't charge it, how much will we
>     loose?


So according to my previous answer, 64kB. I have just noticed that a 
bunch of dynamically allocated chunk fields are not accounted either, 
which again according to Claude represent 2.3kB. I don't have much 
experience in accounting but that's far from negligible right? Which 
amount are we keen to lose to make the code simpler (or for other reasons)?


>
> 3. What would be side effect on assuming that obj_exts is on the same node as
>     the given chunk?


Given the size of obj_exts, overcharging one node while undercharging 
others?

To conclude, you're right, I did not dive deep enough into the metadata 
sizes, I'll fix that.

Thanks,

Alex

series_items.subject: [PATCH 3/8] mm: percpu: Split memcg charging and kmem accounting
series_items.patch_summary: This patch splits memory group (memcg) charging and kernel memory (kmem) accounting into two separate steps to improve per-memcg-per-node kmem accounting. The first step, obj_cgroup_precharge(), reserves pages for memcg limit enforcement without updating kmem stats. The second step, obj_cgroup_account_kmem(), accounts kmem after the allocation succeeds and places any remaining sub-page remainder into the object's stock. This decoupling allows for temporary overcharging of memcg but ensures that kmem accounting is accurate in the long run.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 4/8] mm: memcontrol: track MEMCG_KMEM per NUMA node
series_items.patch_summary: This patch removes the MEMCG_KMEM tracking and instead introduces per-node obj_cgroup objects to track slab and kmem statistics for each NUMA node. The goal is to enable proper per-memcg-per-node accounting, which will be implemented in subsequent patches. Currently, this patch only sets up the necessary infrastructure without converting existing users of MEMCG_KMEM.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 5/8] mm: memcontrol: per-node kmem accounting for page charges
series_items.patch_summary: This patch updates the __memcg_kmem_charge_page function to use per-node object groups for correct NUMA attribution of kernel memory. The change involves introducing a new variable nid_objcg, which is used to get the object group associated with the node where the page resides. This ensures that kernel memory charges are attributed correctly based on the node where the page is allocated.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 6/8] mm: slab: per-node kmem accounting for slab
series_items.patch_summary: This patch updates the slab hook to use per-node object cgroups for accurate NUMA attribution of kernel memory usage (NR_KMEM). It achieves this by introducing a new nid_objcg variable, which retrieves the per-node object cgroup based on the node ID of the slab. The patch modifies several functions within the memcontrol.c file to utilize the nid_objcg instead of the global objcg. This change ensures that kernel memory usage is attributed correctly to each NUMA node, providing a more accurate picture of system resource utilization.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 7/8] mm: percpu: per-node kmem accounting using local credit
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces per-node kmem accounting using a local credit system to handle precharged pages in the percpu memory allocation code. The previous approach used obj_stock, which is no longer suitable due to decoupling of memcg charging and kmem accounting. The new approach ensures that enough charged pages are available for allocation by maintaining a node-specific credit that gets refilled into the stock when necessary.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 8/8] mm: zswap: per-node kmem accounting for zswap/zsmalloc
series_items.patch_summary: This patch updates the zswap and zsmalloc modules to use per-node object cgroup for kernel memory accounting, ensuring that compressed page charges are attributed to the correct NUMA node. However, it does not correctly handle entries that span multiple pages, which will be addressed in a separate series by Joshua Hahn. The patch introduces a new function `zs_handle_to_nid` to determine the NUMA node associated with a given zsmalloc handle and updates the `zswap_store_page` function to use per-node object cgroup for charging. This change is necessary to accurately account for memory usage on systems with multiple NUMA nodes.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series introduces per-memcg-per-node kernel memory accounting to associate memcg and physical memory usage. It builds on recent changes to obj_cgroup, which ties object groups to their NUMA node. The patches split kmem charging and accounting for percpu allocations, and convert other kmem users such as slab and zswap to use the new per-node accounting. This allows for more accurate tracking of kernel memory usage at a per-node level.

review_comments.author: Joshua Hahn
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Joshua Hahn expressed concerns about the performance impact of the patch, specifically suggesting that expensive operations would benefit from benchmarking to demonstrate kernel allocation overhead.

Reviewer Joshua Hahn expressed concerns about the performance implications of splitting obj_cgroup to be per-node, which would invalidate the obj_stock cache and lead to increased atomic operations. He also questioned the need for iterating over num_possible_cpus() x pages_per_alloc.
review_comments.raw_body:
Hello Alex,

Thank you for your work!

Overall I think the direction makes sense to me. Pre-overcharging makes sense to
me as an approach, we would much rather overaccount than underaccount and
later have to breach limits.

I do have some concerns on performance, though. Namely, I think there are
some expensive operations that I think would benefit from some performane
benchmarking with this patch added (maybe some simple microbenchmarks that
demonstrates kernel allocation overhead could be useful).

---

to do with iterating over num_possible_cpus() x pages_per_alloc, which
doesn't seem trivial to me.

Another concern that I see is the stock credit system. Maybe we could be
bypassing the stock check leading to more time spent doing the atomic
operations.

obj_stock caches a single obj_cgroup, which means that if we split the objcg
to be per-node (in patch 6), then the obj_stock basically gets invalidated
every operation since we iterate over more objcgs (even though we are in
the same logical objcg). Maybe I'm missing something?

I haven't taken a deep look at the implementation details but just wanted to
raise some high level items that I noticed. Of course, all of these concerns
are just theoretical, if you can show that the performance delta is not
noticable then all of my concerns don't matter.

I also want to talk more about the local credit system but let's first see
what the numbers are first.

Thanks again, Alex. And I really like patch 2 because it is a solution to
a problem that I ran into in my percpu tracking series that I couldn't think
of before! Thank you for solving my problem too : -)

Have a great day!
Joshua

review_comments.author: Joshua Hahn
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer expressed overall positive sentiment, but noted that he is looking forward to seeing the actual numbers and no specific technical concerns were raised.

Reviewer Joshua Hahn expressed concern about potential performance issues in the patch's per-node kmem accounting implementation when dealing with nested stock systems, specifically obj_cgroup stock and local credit 'stock'. He suggested optimizing the stock refill path by precharging all pages, then iterating over nid_objcgs to perform atomic operations.

Reviewer expressed general approval and appreciation for the patch series without raising any specific technical concerns or objections.
sentiment_signals: looking forward to seeing actual numbers
review_comments.raw_body:
Hi Alex,

That sounds great with me : -) Looking forward to the numbers!

---

So in my initial scan of the patch 7 I had a concern that if we have a nested
stock system (obj_cgroup stock and local credit "stock"), then we could
incur more work if these are out of sync; do extra work in the stock refill
path in obj_cgroup_precharge, and then do extra work on top in the loop
within the pcpu_memcg_post_alloc_hook (obj_cgroup_account_kmem does the
charging atomically I think).

So what I mean is, I'm not sure what the "size" is typically for
pcpu_memcg_post_alloc_hook. But it might be a worthwhile optimization to
do precharge all the pages, then for each cpu iterate over the pages to
figure out how many pages are used per nid (doing just math, not actually
doing the atomic adds), and then outside both of these loops just iterate
over every nid_objcg once to perform the atomic operation.

Maybe this is needed or not (depending on how big "size" typically is
and whether we go from doing O(1000) atomic adds --> O(10) or some
big reduction, but I just wanted to toss it out there as something that
could potentially be expensive.

---

Sounds like a great idea! Thanks again Alex, have a great day! : -)
Joshua

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v2] mm/page_alloc: fix defrag_mode for non-reclaimable allocations

version_history.subject: [PATCH] mm/page_alloc: fix defrag_mode for non-reclaimable allocations
[PATCH v2] mm/page_alloc: fix defrag_mode for non-reclaimable allocations

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch addresses a fragmentation issue in the Linux kernel's page allocator when defrag_mode is enabled and non-reclaimable allocations (e.g., GFP_ATOMIC) are made. The problem arises because these allocations cannot invoke direct reclaim or compaction, leading to SLUB allocation failures under network-heavy workloads. The patch clears ALLOC_NOFRAGMENT for such allocations that request kswapd reclaim but cannot do direct reclaim themselves.

review_comments.author: Andrew Morton
review_comments.summary: Identified a potential fragmentation issue in the page allocator when defrag_mode is enabled and non-reclaimable allocations are made. Asked how serious this issue is to users running real-world workloads.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Wed, 20 May 2026 12:22:28 +0000 Dmitry Ilvokhin <d@ilvokhin.com> wrote:

> When defrag_mode is enabled, ALLOC_NOFRAGMENT is enforced to prevent
> migratetype fallbacks and keep pageblocks clean. The allocator relies on
> reclaim and compaction to free pages of the correct type before allowing
> fallback as a last resort.
> 
> However, non-reclaimable allocations such as GFP_ATOMIC cannot invoke
> direct reclaim or compaction. With defrag_mode=1, these allocations hit
> the !can_direct_reclaim bailout in __alloc_pages_slowpath() with
> ALLOC_NOFRAGMENT still set, and fail without ever attempting a fallback.
> 
> This causes a large number of SLUB allocation failures for
> skbuff_head_cache under network-heavy workloads, despite free memory
> being available in other migratetype freelists.

That sounds painful.

> Clear ALLOC_NOFRAGMENT and retry for allocations that request kswapd
> reclaim but cannot do direct reclaim themselves (GFP_ATOMIC).  Purely
> speculative allocations like GFP_TRANSHUGE_LIGHT that don't set
> __GFP_KSWAPD_RECLAIM are left to fail, since they have reasonable
> fallbacks and should not cause fragmentation.

How serious is this to our users when running real-world workloads?

> Fixes: e3aa7df331bc ("mm: page_alloc: defrag_mode")
> 
> Signed-off-by: Dmitry Ilvokhin <d@ilvokhin.com>
> Acked-by: Johannes Weiner <hannes@cmpxchg.org>

review_comments.author: Andrew Morton
review_comments.summary: Acknowledged the severity of the issue, but questioned whether it's a disaster and suggested that fast-tracking the patch into 7.1 might not be necessary.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Fri, 22 May 2026 13:05:36 +0000 Dmitry Ilvokhin <d@ilvokhin.com> wrote:

> > How serious is this to our users when running real-world workloads?
> 
> We observed it on a few of the Meta workloads that adopted
> defrag_mode=1.
> 
> For the service under load there were 85509 SLUB allocation failures
> messages in dmesg within 2 hours. All of them are GFP_ATOMIC allocations
> for skbuff_head_cache, despite free pages being available in other
> migratetype freelists (~13 GB free).

For a single machine, I assume.

> Since it is networking path from the practical point of view, this means
> dropped packets, failed RPC requests, tail latency spikes and overall
> service degradation.

OK, thanks.   I assume 12 failures per second isn't a disaster, and that
there's no need to fast-track this into 7.1?

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v5 00/21] nfsd: add support for CB_NOTIFY callbacks in directory delegations

version_history.subject: [PATCH v4 00/21] nfsd: add support for CB_NOTIFY callbacks in directory delegations
[PATCH v5 00/21] nfsd: add support for CB_NOTIFY callbacks in directory delegations

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 01/21] nfsd: check fl_lmops in nfsd_breaker_owns_lease()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch modifies the nfsd_breaker_owns_lease() function to quickly check if a lease is an nfsd-managed lease by verifying that its fl_lmops field matches the nfsd_lease_mng_ops structure. If it does, the function returns true without further checks. This optimization improves performance by reducing unnecessary computations for nfsd leases.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 02/21] nfsd: add protocol support for CB_NOTIFY
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds support for the CB_NOTIFY protocol to the nfsd kernel module. It updates various header files, including nfs4_1.x and uapi/linux/nfs4.h, to include definitions for NFSv4 constants such as NFS4_VERIFIER_SIZE and NFS4_FHSIZE. The patch also regenerates XDR (External Data Representation) files, which caused conflicts with existing kernel API definitions. To resolve these conflicts, the patch removes duplicate definitions from nfs4.h and uapi/nfs4 header. Userspace consumers who require these constants should obtain them from more authoritative sources.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 03/21] nfs_common: add new NOTIFY4_* flags proposed in RFC8881bis
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds new NOTIFY4_* flags to the NFSv4 protocol as proposed in RFC8881bis. The new flags include NOTIFY4_GFLAG_EXTEND, NOTIFY4_AUFLAG_VALID, and others that extend the functionality of the GET_DIR_DELEGATION operation. These changes are necessary to support the updated protocol specification. The patch modifies several files, including xdr/nfs4_1.x, nfsd/nfs4xdr_gen.c, and include/linux/sunrpc/xdrgen/nfs4_1.h.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 04/21] nfsd: allow nfsd to get a dir lease with an ignore mask
series_items.patch_summary: This patch allows the NFS daemon to obtain a directory lease with an ignore mask, enabling it to selectively ignore certain notification types when requesting a directory lease. The patch introduces a new function `nfsd_notify_to_ignore` that maps notification types to corresponding flags in the file lease structure. This change is made to improve the flexibility and customization of NFS directory leases.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 05/21] nfsd: update the fsnotify mark when setting or removing a dir delegation
series_items.patch_summary: This patch updates the fsnotify mark when setting or removing a directory delegation in NFSd. It introduces a new helper function, `nfsd_fsnotify_recalc_mask`, which recalculates the mask on the nfsd_file's fsnotify_mark based on the current directory delegations on an inode. The function is called when adding or removing directory delegations to ensure that notifications are properly set up for ignored delegation events. This patch addresses a potential issue where the fsnotify mark was not being updated correctly, leading to incorrect notification behavior.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 06/21] nfsd: make nfsd4_callback_ops->prepare operation bool return
series_items.patch_summary: This patch modifies the nfsd4_callback_ops->prepare operation to return a boolean value indicating whether callback processing should continue or not. The change is necessary because, in the case of CB_NOTIFY operations, allocation failures should cause callback processing to stop. The patch updates several functions that implement the prepare operation for different types of callbacks to return true if processing should continue and false otherwise.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 07/21] nfsd: add callback encoding and decoding linkages for CB_NOTIFY
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds routines for encoding and decoding CB_NOTIFY messages in the NFS server (nfsd). It introduces new functions to handle the encoding and decoding of these messages, which are generated by the xdrgen tool. The patch also updates the state.h file with new definitions related to notification events. This change is part of a larger series of patches that aim to improve the performance and functionality of the NFS server.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 08/21] nfsd: use RCU to protect fi_deleg_file
series_items.patch_summary: This patch protects the fi_deleg_file pointer in the NFS server code using RCU (Read-Copy Update) to prevent concurrent access issues during delegation teardown. It annotates the pointer with __rcu and converts all accessors to use proper RCU primitives, such as rcu_assign_pointer() for stores and rcu_dereference_protected() for reads under a lock or where stability is guaranteed. This patch prepares for a subsequent change that will safely acquire a reference from the CB_NOTIFY callback path without holding the fi_lock.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 09/21] nfsd: add data structures for handling CB_NOTIFY
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds data structures and callback operations to support directory delegation CB_NOTIFY in the NFS server. It introduces two new structs, nfsd_notify_event and nfsd4_cb_notify, which carry fsnotify events and per-delegation state for notification handling, respectively. The patch also refactors alloc_init_deleg into a common function with a pluggable sc_free callback and adds alloc_init_dir_deleg to allocate resources needed for CB_NOTIFY encoding. Additionally, it sets up skeleton nfsd4_cb_notify_ops with done and release handlers that will be filled in when the notification path is wired up.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 10/21] nfsd: add notification handlers for dir events
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds support for directory change events in the NFS server (nfsd). It introduces a new notification handler and creates a CB_NOTIFY request to handle these events. When a directory is created, it sets up a callback to handle notifications and allocates an nfsd_notify_event object. If any delegation is affected by the event, it recalls them if necessary. The patch modifies several files in the fs/nfsd directory to implement this functionality.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 11/21] nfsd: add tracepoint to dir_event handler
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a tracepoint to the dir_event handler in the NFS server (nfsd) code, providing extra visibility into how fsnotify handlers are being called. The new tracepoint is named nfsd_handle_dir_event and it captures information about the directory event, including the mask of events triggered, the inode involved, and the name of the file or directory affected. This addition will help with debugging and understanding the behavior of the NFS server when handling directory events.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 12/21] nfsd: apply the notify mask to the delegation when requested
series_items.patch_summary: This patch modifies the NFS server to apply a notify mask to directory delegations when requested by the client. If the client enables notifications, the server sets the appropriate return mask in gddr_notification[0] to ensure lease acquisition sets the correct ignore mask. The server also checks if the client has set NOTIFY4_GFLAG_EXTEND and only offers notifications if it is enabled, as classic notifications require directory offset information which nfsd does not provide without this flag.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 13/21] nfsd: add helper to marshal a fattr4 from completed args
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a helper function to marshal the attr_vals field of an fattr4 from completed arguments in the NFSv4 server. The new function, nfsd4_encode_attr_vals, breaks out the encoding of attr_vals into its own separate function, making the code more modular and easier to maintain.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 14/21] nfsd: allow nfsd4_encode_fattr4_change() to work with no export
series_items.patch_summary: This patch allows the nfsd4_encode_fattr4_change() function to work even when there is no export available, which can occur in certain callback scenarios such as CB_NOTIFY. The change ensures that the function does not attempt to access a svc_export when it's not present, preventing potential errors. This modification should be safe because NFS will not grant delegations on the V4 root anyway.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 15/21] nfsd: send basic file attributes in CB_NOTIFY
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds the ability to send basic file attributes in CB_NOTIFY events, including inode information that can be acquired via GETATTR. The attributes sent include type, change time, size, file ID, mode, number of links, raw device, space used, access time, metadata time, and modify time. This allows clients to receive updated attribute information without needing to issue a separate CB_GETATTR request.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 16/21] nfsd: allow encoding a filehandle into fattr4 without a svc_fh
series_items.patch_summary: This patch allows the encoding of a filehandle into fattr4 without requiring a svc_fh. It achieves this by adding a new 'fhandle' field to struct nfsd4_fattr_args and copying the filehandle into it from the svc_fh in cases where the filehandle is needed but a svc_fh is not available, such as in CB_NOTIFY callbacks.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 17/21] nfsd: add a fi_connectable flag to struct nfs4_file
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new flag, fi_connectable, to the nfs4_file structure in the NFSv4 state management code. The flag indicates whether a filehandle should be encoded as connectable or not, based on whether subtree checking is enabled for the underlying export. This change addresses a specific scenario where a CB_NOTIFY operation requires knowledge of whether to use connectable filehandles.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 18/21] nfsd: add the filehandle to returned attributes in CB_NOTIFY
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new routine to compose a filehandle for use in CB_NOTIFY operations, where there is no export to consult. The routine uses the parent filehandle and nfs4_file to fill out the fhandle field in the nfsd4_fattr_args structure. This allows the filehandle to be returned as an attribute in CB_NOTIFY responses.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 19/21] nfsd: properly track requested child attributes
series_items.patch_summary: This patch improves the way NFSv4 server handles child attributes in delegations by tracking the union of requested and supported attributes, and only encoding the attributes in that union when sending add/remove/rename updates. This change ensures that only valid attributes are sent to clients, preventing potential errors or inconsistencies. The patch modifies several files in the NFSv4 codebase to implement this new behavior, including nfsd/nfs4proc.c, nfsd/nfs4state.c, and nfsd/nfs4xdr.c.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 20/21] nfsd: track requested dir attributes
series_items.patch_summary: This patch updates the NFS server to track the union of requested and supported directory attributes in the delegation. This change will be used later to ensure that only the relevant attributes are encoded when sending add, remove, or rename operations. The patch modifies several files, including nfsd/nfs4proc.c and nfsd/nfs4state.c, to update the handling of directory attributes and notifications.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 21/21] nfsd: add support to CB_NOTIFY for dir attribute changes
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds support to CB_NOTIFY for dir attribute changes in the NFS server. When a client requests notifications for directory attribute changes, the server will send these notifications alongside other events such as add/remove/rename operations. The server still recalls the delegation on a SETATTR operation, so these notifications are only sent for changes to child directories. The patch modifies two files: nfsd/nfs4state.c and fs/nfsd/nfs4xdr.c, adding new code to handle dir attribute change notifications.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series adds support for CB_NOTIFY callbacks in directory delegations to the NFS server, enabling clients to register interest in a directory and receive notifications about changes within it without losing their lease.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton
review_comments.summary: The patch series addresses several issues related to directory delegation and CB_NOTIFY callbacks, including proper handling of NULL pointers, RCU protection, and data structure additions. The patches also refactor alloc_init_deleg() into a common __alloc_init_deleg() base with a pluggable sc_free callback.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH] nfsd: reset write verifier on deferred writeback errors

version_history.subject: [PATCH] nfsd: reset write verifier on deferred writeback errors

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in the NFS server where deferred writeback errors were not properly handled, leading to silent data loss. The issue arises when clients hold UNSTABLE write data under the current verifier and then COMMIT, only to receive the unchanged verifier back and conclude their data is durable. To fix this, the patch adds calls to commit_reset_write_verifier() at two error sites in nfsd_vfs_write() and nfsd_commit(), ensuring that the server's write verifier is properly rotated on deferred writeback errors. This change aligns with existing patterns in the code and ensures compliance with the UNSTABLE+COMMIT durability contract.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v3 00/10] fuse: add io-uring buffer pools and zero-copy

version_history.subject: [PATCH v3 00/10] fuse: add io-uring buffer pools and zero-copy

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 01/10] fuse: separate next request fetching from sending logic
series_items.patch_summary: This patch separates the logic of fetching the next request from sending it, making the control flow clearer and reducing unnecessary parameter passing. It removes duplicated code in fuse_uring_send_next_to_ring() and introduces a new function fuse_uring_get_next_fuse_req() to fetch the next request. The send operation is now handled by a separate function fuse_uring_send(). This change simplifies the logic for handling requests and improves maintainability.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 02/10] fuse: refactor io-uring header copying to ring
series_items.patch_summary: This patch refactors the logic for copying headers to a ring in the FUSE (Filesystem in Userspace) io-uring implementation. It extracts the header copying into a new function called copy_header_to_ring(), which centralizes error handling and rate-limited logging, making the code more clear and maintainable. The patch modifies several functions that interact with the ring, replacing direct calls to copy_to_user() with calls to the new function.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 03/10] fuse: refactor io-uring header copying from ring
series_items.patch_summary: This patch refactors the logic for copying headers from a ring in the fuse kernel module. It extracts this logic into a new function called copy_header_from_ring(), which makes it clearer and more centralized, and also handles errors and rate-limited logging. This change is part of a larger series of patches to improve the io-uring functionality in the fuse module.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 04/10] fuse: use enum types for header copying
series_items.patch_summary: This patch improves the interface for copying headers in the FUSE (Filesystem in Userspace) implementation by introducing enum types to identify which part of the header needs to be copied. This change will simplify both kernel-space and user-space header addresses copying when buffer rings are added, making it easier to manage and maintain the code.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 05/10] fuse: refactor setting up copy state for payload copying
series_items.patch_summary: This patch refactors the code for setting up copy state in the FUSE implementation of dev_uring by introducing a new helper function `setup_fuse_copy_state`. This function encapsulates the logic for importing user buffers and initializing the copy state. The existing code is simplified and made more modular, reducing duplication and improving maintainability.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 06/10] fuse: use named constants for io-uring iovec indices
series_items.patch_summary: This patch replaces magic indices 0 and 1 in the iovec array with named constants FUSE_URING_IOV_HEADERS and FUSE_URING_IOV_PAYLOAD, making the code self-documenting and preparing for future buffer ring support. The patch modifies several functions in the fuse module to use these new constants instead of hardcoded indices. This change improves code readability and maintainability by eliminating magic numbers and making it easier to understand the purpose of each index.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 07/10] fuse: add io-uring buffer pools (implemented as buffer ring)
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds support for buffer pools in the fuse io-uring implementation, allowing the server to pass a contiguous allocation of buffers that can be used on demand by the kernel. This decouples the number of entries from the number of payload buffers required, reducing memory usage and enabling future optimizations such as incremental buffer consumption. The server must set the FUSE_URING_BUFPOOL flag during registration and provide the payload buffer pool through an iovec payload in the first sqe. The kernel manages buffer selection and recycling using a buffer ring data structure.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 08/10] fuse: support registered buffers as buffer pools in io-uring
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds support for using registered buffers as buffer pools in io-uring, allowing servers to use the same pinned pages for subsequent I/O operations and reducing overhead. Registered buffers require enabling the FUSE_URING_BUFPOOL flag and setting the FUSE_URING_REGISTERED_BUFFERS init flag during registration. The patch also includes benchmark results showing a 10% throughput improvement for direct randreads and other improvements for different types of reads.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 09/10] fuse: add zero-copy over io-uring
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds zero-copy support to the fuse io-uring implementation in the Linux kernel. It eliminates memory copies between userspace, the kernel, and the fuse server for read/write operations by allowing the server to directly access client pages or page cache folios without copying data through an intermediary buffer. The patch requires CAP_SYS_ADMIN and FUSE_URING_REGISTERED_BUFFERS capabilities and is gated behind these requirements. The zero-copy write path involves registering relevant pages in the server's io-uring registered buffer table, operating on these pages using fixed buffer operations, and unregistering them when the request completes.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 10/10] docs: fuse: add io-uring buffer pool and zero-copy documentation
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds documentation to the Linux kernel's FUSE (Filesystem in Userspace) module for using buffer pools and zero-copy functionality with io-uring. The documentation explains how to use buffer pools, which decouple the relationship between entries and payload buffers, allowing for more efficient memory usage. It also describes how the server manages the buffer pool through a buffer ring, enabling features like incremental buffer consumption and multiple buffer sizes per ring. This patch aims to improve performance by reducing unnecessary copies of data between kernel and user space.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series adds buffer pools and zero-copy functionality to the fuse io-uring implementation, allowing servers to pass in a managed buffer pool that reduces memory usage requirements and enables future optimizations. It also allows for direct access to client pages or page cache folios without copying data through an intermediary buffer, requiring CAP_SYS_ADMIN privileges. The changes are based on registered buffers and zero-copy, which can provide significant throughput improvements depending on the server's per-request latency and backing I/O characteristics.

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary: The author is addressing a concern about duplicated logic in the code, specifically in the fuse_uring_send_next_to_ring() function. They simplified the logic by removing this function and instead calling fuse_uring_prepare_send() directly from fuse_uring_get_next_fuse_req(). The change reduces unnecessary parameter passing and makes the control flow clearer.

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about the copy_to_user() logic being scattered throughout the code, and moved it into a new function called copy_header_to_ring(). This centralizes error handling and rate-limited logging.

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about the copy_from_user() logic being unclear and error-prone by moving it into a new function, copy_header_from_ring(), which centralizes error handling and rate-limited logging.

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about the header copying interface, agreeing to improve it by using enum types to identify which part of the header needs to be copied. This will simplify both kernel-space and user-space header addresses copying when buffer rings are added.

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about the import_ubuf() function being called multiple times in the code, and instead created a new helper function setup_fuse_copy_state() to encapsulate this logic.

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about magic indices in the iovec array by replacing them with named constants FUSE_URING_IOV_HEADERS and FUSE_URING_IOV_PAYLOAD, making the code self-documenting and preparing for buffer ring support.

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about the tight coupling between ents and buffers in fuse io-uring by explaining that they have decoupled them, allowing servers to pass in a buffer pool that the kernel will use for servicing ents/requests. The server must set the FUSE_URING_BUFPOOL flag during registration and provide the payload buffer pool through the iovec payload in the first sqe. The author described the internal buffer ring data structure used for managing the buffer pool, including how buffers are selected and recycled.

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about the overhead of pinning/unpinning user pages and translating virtual addresses per i/o request, explaining that registered buffers eliminate this overhead by pinning pages upfront. The author provided details on how to use registered buffers, including setting specific flags in the registration sqe and following standard protocol for using registered buffers.

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about the zero-copy implementation, specifically the request flow and registration/unregistration path for page-backed and non-page-backed arguments. The author provided detailed explanations of the zero-copy read and write paths, including the use of io_buffer_register_bvec() and fuse_uring_set_up_zero_copy(). They confirmed that the server can directly access client pages or page cache folios without copying data through an intermediary buffer, but noted that this requires CAP_SYS_ADMIN privileges. The author also acknowledged that some requests may have both page-backed and non-page-backed arguments, which are handled differently in the zero-copy implementation.

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary: The author is addressing reviewer Bernd's request to review the series, and acknowledges that the logic remains the same as previous versions but has renamed it to 'buffer pool' for uapi-facing reasons.
review_comments.raw_body:
Just to reiterate, the logic is the same between this version and the
previous versions. I just renamed it to "buffer pool" to decouple the
implementation detail from the concept since it's uapi facing. The
buffer pool is managed as a buffer ring internally.

Bernd, it would be much appreciated if you could take a look at this
series when you have the time. I'm targeting the June merge window and
the io-uring dependent changes have already been queued up in Jens's
tree since the last merge window.

Thanks,
Joanne

review_comments.author: Bernd Schubert
review_comments.summary:
reviewer noted that the patch does not handle the case where a buffer is already in use by another io-uring operation and requested a check to be added to prevent this

Reviewer questioned whether header pinning is still necessary, noting that if it's not done, the application cannot write into fuse-server buffers and headers without pre-pinned pages, which would lead to a context switch for all sync requests.

Reviewer suggested an alternative design where the payload is registered per ring entry and only the header becomes a buffer ring, allowing for dynamic registration of entries without a payload and reducing memory usage.
review_comments.raw_body:
Hi Joanne,

On 5/22/26 23:14, Joanne Koong wrote:

---

short question, I didn't check the code is header pinning still
*possible*? The problem if we do not pin headers is that
io_uring_cmd_complete_in_task() cannot be avoided, as the application
cannot write into fuse-server buffers and headers without pre-pinned
pages. And io_uring_cmd_complete_in_task() basically means a context
switch for all sync requests.

---

I will try and right is probably a good time, almost through with
libfuse sync-init (remaining are bugs), Darricks series is also almost
ready. Although I don't promise this weekend - reserved for Darrick and
other libfuse changes.

One piece I had been pondering a bit about, though did not dive into the
code yet.
What is if payload would be registered per ring entry (as without your
changes) and just the header would become a buffer ring (so that could
be pinned that way without loosing one page per entry)?  For zero-copy
we could register entries without a payload? Advantage here would be
that that header buffer would determine max number of entries, only
little memory would be needed for over-subscribed entries, but entries
themselves could be registered dynamically on demand.


Thanks,
Bernd

Thanks,
Bernd

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary:
Author acknowledged that header pinning was removed from the patchset and is being addressed separately, confirming it's not a concern for this specific series.

Author agrees that payload buffers should be decoupled from ring entries to reduce memory usage, but does not provide a clear plan for addressing this issue.

The author addressed a concern about header pinning by explaining that the headers are stored in a single contiguous region, thus avoiding the need for separate pages per entry.

The author is addressing a concern about zero-copy functionality in the patch series, specifically that many servers may not be able to use it due to CAP_SYS_ADMIN requirements. The author acknowledges this limitation and suggests minimizing memory consumption for the non-zero-copy path through buffer pools.
review_comments.raw_body:
Hi Bernd,

Header pinning was dropped from this patchset. I have a separate
branch that has header pinning alongside the changes for avoiding the
fuse io_uring_cmd_complete_in_task() deferral. I moved the header
pinning changes out of this patchset as it doesn't tie in with getting
perf improvements from using registered buffers, unlike payload buffer
pinning.

---

I think payload buffers must be decoupled from ring entries if we want
to reduce memory usage in the non-zero-copy path.

---

For the header pinning changes in my branch, the headers are one
contiguous region (not per-entry), which circumvents having to
allocate one page per entry.

---

For zero-copy, entries still may have payloads (though the buffers
will definitely not need to be as big as the max payload size!),
either for the non-zero-copyable args or if the request's args aren't
page-backed. Unfortunately I think many servers may not be able to use
zero-copy due to the CAP_SYS_ADMIN requirements, so I think it'd be
ideal to minimize the memory consumption needed for the non-zero-copy
path, through the buffer pool for the payload buffers.

I hope I'm interpreting your question correctly - if not, please let me know!

Thanks,
Joanne

review_comments.author: Bernd Schubert
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Bernd Schubert questioned the need for registering ring entries without buffers and suggested that dynamic registration of ring entries based on demand would be a better approach.
review_comments.raw_body:
Well, my problem is still why would we need to register ring entries if
they don't have buffers - why not just register less ring entries then.

At least I do not see the use case for ring entries without buffers.
And that is exactly where my question about keeping the possibility for
dynamic ring entry registration comes from. If ring entries register the
buffers themselves, every ring could be set up with a single ring entry
first and only once ring entries start to get used, libfuse could
register more based on demand.

Thanks,
Bernd

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed concerns about the buffer pool implementation, explaining that entries can be used for servicing requests even if there are fewer buffers than entries. They also highlighted the benefits of decoupling ents and buffers, including supporting multiple buffer sizes per ring and eliminating the need for payload buffers in certain requests.
review_comments.raw_body:
Entries may be used for servicing requests even if there are less
buffers than the number of entries. Most requests don't use the full
payload buffer size, so for example with incremental buffer
consumption, this would allow multiple entries to use non-overlapping
regions of the same buffer concurrently. As well, if we want to
support multiple buffer sizes per ring, there shouldn't be a 1:1
mapping between ents and buffers. There's also some requests that
don't need to use payload buffers at all (eg flush, fsync, rmdir,
etc).  As well for registered buffers / buffer pinning, it is more
optimal for the buffer pool to be allocated contiguously and pinned
contiguously rather than passed in one by one as a 1:1 entry:buffer
mapping.

Thanks,
Joanne

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH v3 00/10] fuse: add io-uring buffer pools and zero-copy
Re: [PATCH v3 00/10] fuse: add io-uring buffer pools and zero-copy
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: This patch series adds buffer pools and zero-copy functionality to the fuse io-uring implementation, allowing servers to pass in a managed buffer pool that reduces memory usage requirements and enables future optimizations. It also allows for direct access to client pages or page cache folios without copying data through an intermediary buffer, requiring CAP_SYS_ADMIN privileges. The changes are based on registered buffers and zero-copy, which can provide significant throughput improvements depending on the server's per-request latency and backing I/O characteristics.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH 7/7 v2] mm/memcontrol: remove unused memcg_stock code

version_history.subject: [PATCH 7/7 v2] mm/memcontrol: remove unused memcg_stock code

series_items.subject: [PATCH 6/7 v2] mm/memcontrol: optimize stock usage for cgroup v2
series_items.patch_summary: This patch optimizes memory usage in cgroup v2 by draining the stock of parent cgroups when a child is created, allowing other memcgs to reuse those pages. This is done because direct charges to non-leaf cgroups are rare in cgroup v2, and excess memory in parent cgroups can be wasted. The optimization does not apply to cgroup v1, where tasks can be attached to any cgroup in the hierarchy.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 5/7 v2] mm/memcontrol: optimize memsw stock for cgroup v1
series_items.patch_summary: This patch optimizes the way memory usage is tracked in cgroup v1 by introducing a new stock for the memsw page counter, which is charged independently from the memory page counter. This change improves caching and reduces the number of hierarchical charges required, especially in scenarios where both counters are over their limit. As a result, reported memsw usage may temporarily be lower than memory usage due to batching of stock charges.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 4/7 v2] mm/memcontrol: convert memcg to use page_counter_stock
series_items.patch_summary: This patch converts the memcg (memory cgroup) code to use page_counter_stock instead of its own handling logic. This change removes special casing for avoiding refilling and flushing of old stock, allows failure during page_counter_enable_stock() if memory allocation fails, and restructures drain_all_stock to iterate CPUs in an outer loop for more efficient scheduling.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 3/7 v2] mm/page_counter: introduce stock drain APIs
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces two new functions, page_counter_drain_stock_local and page_counter_drain_stock_cpu, which are used to drain the stock of a page counter. The local function takes a lock to serialize against concurrent charges, while the CPU function does not take a lock because it is only called from the CPU hotplug path where the CPU is dead and cannot have any more charges. These functions replace the existing memcg stock draining functions.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 2/7 v2] mm/page_counter: use page_counter_stock in page_counter_try_charge
series_items.patch_summary: This patch updates the page_counter_try_charge function to use the page_counter_stock feature when available. It first checks if there is enough stock to fulfill the charge request, and if so, charges it directly from the stock without hierarchical charging. If not, it attempts a greedy hierarchical charge, and if successful, tries to add any surplus pages to the stock. The patch preserves the existing semantics of the stock handling logic in try_charge_memcg.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 1/7 v2] mm/page_counter: introduce per-page_counter stock
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces a per-page_counter stock to the Linux kernel's memory management system, allowing each page counter to have its own cache of pre-charged pages. This addresses two issues: the random eviction problem caused by the current implementation's limit on the number of memcgs that can be cached, and the tight coupling between stock and memcg, which prevents new page counters from using separate stocks. The patch adds a generic per-cpu stock to the struct page_counter and provides functions for enabling and disabling stock on a per-page_counter basis.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 0/7 v2] mm/memcontrol, page_counter: move stock from mem_cgroup to page_counter
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series moves the per-cpu stock from mem_cgroup to page_counter, allowing for more efficient and scalable memory management. The current implementation has limitations, including a 7-memcg-per-cpu slot limit and random evictions of stock when multiple memcgroups are charging on a single CPU. By moving the stock to page_counter, these issues are resolved, enabling independent stock management for additional page_counters in each memcg. This change also simplifies the code and makes it easier to follow. However, there are some tradeoffs, including an increased bound on overcharged memory and additional memory footprint due to a new struct page_counter_stock.

review_comments.author: Joshua Hahn (author)
review_comments.summary: The author is addressing concerns about the current implementation of per-cpu stocks in memcontrol, specifically the random eviction problem and tight coupling with struct mem_cgroup. The author agrees that these issues can be addressed by pushing the concept of stock down to the page_counter level, which would eliminate the 7-memcg-per-cpu slot limit and make enabling separate stock caches for other page_counters simpler.

review_comments.author: Joshua Hahn (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about the page_counter_stock being unused and not enabled on any memcg yet, stating that no functional changes are intended.

review_comments.author: Joshua Hahn (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about the need for synchronization in stock draining, introduced two new functions to drain stock: page_counter_drain_stock_local() and page_counter_drain_stock_cpu(), which take a local lock or not respectively, depending on the context. The author did not explicitly state that they will restructure the code in v2, but the introduction of these new functions suggests that further revision may be necessary.

review_comments.author: Joshua Hahn (author)
review_comments.summary: Author addressed a concern about the potential for stale stock on large machines by explaining that the new design's overcharge bound is nr_leaf_cgroups x nr_cpus x 64 pages, and arguing that this should not be a problem as long as cgroups are actively charging memory. The author also noted that the system can continue to operate with the page counter working without stock and falling back to walking the hierarchy if there is not enough memory to allocate a percpu page_counter_stock.

review_comments.author: Joshua Hahn (author)
review_comments.summary: Author addressed a concern about the lack of a fast path for cgroup v1's memsw check after moving the per-cpu stock to page_counter, explained that introducing an independent stock for memsw provides better caching and avoids the worst-case scenario where both counters must walk two hierarchies. The author confirmed this change will be included in the next version.

review_comments.author: Joshua Hahn (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about wasted memory in parent cgroups due to rare direct charges, and explained that draining the stock when the first child is created can return pages for other memcgs to use. This optimization is specific to cgroup v2, as tasks can be attached to any cgroup in the hierarchy in v1.
review_comments.raw_body:
In cgroup v2, it is unlikely for memcg charges to happen directly on
non-leaf cgroups. There are a few exceptions, such as processes that
have yet to be migrated to children, and tasks that are reparented on
memcg destruction, that charge to the parent.

Because it is rare for parent cgroups to receive direct charges, stock
that remains in them are wasted memory.

Drain parent stocks when the first child is created to return those
pages for other memcgs to use.

This optimization is not for cgroup v1, where tasks can be attached to
any cgroup in the hierarchy, meaning stock can be consumed & refilled
for non-leaf cgroups as well.

Signed-off-by: Joshua Hahn <joshua.hahnjy@gmail.com>
---
 mm/memcontrol.c | 15 +++++++++++++++
 1 file changed, 15 insertions(+)

diff --git a/mm/memcontrol.c b/mm/memcontrol.c
index 06ec4d26cb519..fc1e1b10b6ab6 100644
--- a/mm/memcontrol.c
+++ b/mm/memcontrol.c
@@ -3968,6 +3968,21 @@ static int mem_cgroup_css_online(struct cgroup_subsys_state *css)
 	 */
 	xa_store(&mem_cgroup_private_ids, memcg->id.id, memcg, GFP_KERNEL);
 
+	/*
+	 * It is unlikely for non-leaf memcgs to get direct charges on v2.
+	 * Drain the parent's stock if we are the first child.
+	 */
+	if (cgroup_subsys_on_dfl(memory_cgrp_subsys)) {
+		struct mem_cgroup *parent = parent_mem_cgroup(memcg);
+		int cpu;
+
+		if (parent && !mem_cgroup_is_root(parent) &&
+			      !css_has_online_children(&parent->css)) {
+			for_each_online_cpu(cpu)
+				work_on_cpu(cpu, drain_stock_on_cpu, parent);
+		}
+	}
+
 	return 0;
 offline_kmem:
 	memcg_offline_kmem(memcg);
-- 
2.53.0-Meta

review_comments.author: Joshua Hahn (author)
review_comments.summary: The author acknowledged that the memcg_stock logic has been moved to page_counter_stock, and as a result, all code related to handling memcg_stock can be removed. The obj_stock is untouched and still needed.

review_comments.author: Joshua Hahn (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledges a mistake in patch development process, proposes rebasing on mm-new and sending as v3, indicating a fix is planned for the next version.
review_comments.raw_body:
This iteration was developed and tested on top of mm-stable.
I'm seeing now that Sashiko cannot apply this patch, and I think it expects
it to have been built on top of mm-new.

Reviewers -- would it make sense to rebase this on top of mm-new and
re-send this as a v3, or should I wait for feedback on this cycle
before sending out a new version?

In any case, this could have been avoided if I just developed on top of
mm-new. I'll be mindful to do that in the future.

Thank you everyone,
Joshua

review_comments.author: Andrew Morton
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Andrew Morton suggested resending the patch for Sashiko review and human review, citing the rapidly growing mm-new branch and potential value in parallelizing reviews.
review_comments.raw_body:
Sashiko does attempt various branches, including mm-stable so I'm not
sure what went wrong here.

It's a bit of a crapshoot at this time, as mm-new is still growing like
a weed (19 patches yesterday, 24 so far today).  Slowing way down soon!
I'll be pushing mm-new later this evening and after that it shouldn't change
much for several days because we all take weekends off, don't we?

Convention appears to say "wait a week before resending" but IMO
there's value in parallelizing Sashiko review with human review.  And
it's understandable if some humans aren't very motivated to review
until the AI thing has had a shot at it, because that might result in
alterations.  So I'd say that a resend-for-Sashiko is appropriate.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch removes unused code related to handling memcg_stock in the Linux kernel's memory control module (mm/memcontrol.c). The removal is possible because all relevant logic has been moved to page_counter_stock, and obj_stock remains untouched. The patch preserves FLUSHING_CACHED_CHARGE for use by obj_stock and deletes 183 lines of code from mm/memcontrol.c.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 01/16] mm: decouple protnone helpers from CONFIG_NUMA_BALANCING
series_items.patch_summary: This patch decouples the protnone helpers from CONFIG_NUMA_BALANCING by introducing a new configuration option, CONFIG_ARCH_HAS_PTE_PROTNONE. This allows architectures that support present-but-inaccessible page table entries (protnone PTEs) to use them independently of NUMA balancing. The patch updates various architecture-specific code and Kconfig files to reflect this change, enabling six architectures to select the protnone infrastructure without relying on CONFIG_NUMA_BALANCING.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 02/16] mm: rename uffd-wp PTE bit macros to uffd
series_items.patch_summary: This patch renames the uffd-wp PTE bit macros to simply 'uffd' in various architectures, including x86 and arm64. The rename is done to reflect that the bit is used for userfaultfd tracking, regardless of whether it's write-protect or read-write-protect. This change is purely mechanical and does not alter any behavior. It prepares the ground for a second consumer of this PTE bit, which will be used by userfaultfd RWP (read-write-protect).

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 03/16] mm: rename uffd-wp PTE accessors to uffd
series_items.patch_summary: This patch renames kernel-internal functions and variables related to the uffd-wp PTE bit, which is reused for marking access-tracking PTEs in addition to write protection. The renaming is mechanical and does not change behavior. The changes affect various architecture-specific header files, mm code, and userfaultfd code. The goal of this patch is to simplify the naming conventions and make it clear that the meaning of the uffd-wp bit depends on the VMA flag (WP or RWP).

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 04/16] mm: add VM_UFFD_RWP VMA flag
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new VMA flag, VM_UFFD_RWP, to enable read-write protection for userfaultfd. The flag is gated on the CONFIG_USERFAULTFD_RWP configuration option and is used in conjunction with the userfaultfd system call to trap both read and write accesses to protected memory ranges. The patch also introduces helper functions, updates documentation, and adds a new entry to the smaps

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 05/16] mm: add MM_CP_UFFD_RWP change_protection() flag
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds two new flags to the change_protection() function in the Linux kernel: MM_CP_UFFD_RWP and MM_CP_UFFD_RWP_RESOLVE. These flags are used for userfaultfd (UFFD) read-write protection, which is a mechanism that allows userspace to protect memory ranges from being accessed by other processes. The MM_CP_UFFD_RWP flag installs PAGE_NONE with the uffd PTE bit set, while the MM_CP_UFFD_RWP_RESOLVE flag restores the original vma->vm_page_prot and clears the uffd bit. This patch also updates several kernel functions to support these new flags, including change_pte_range(), change_huge_pmd(), and hugetlb_change_protection(). The goal of this patch is to provide a consistent and efficient way for UFFD to protect memory ranges in the kernel.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 06/16] mm: preserve RWP marker across PTE rewrites
series_items.patch_summary: This patch ensures that the RWP (Read-Only Write-Protection) marker is preserved across PTE rewrites in various kernel paths, including swap, device-exclusive, migration, fork, and mprotect operations. Without this preservation, the RWP tracking could be silently dropped, leading to unexpected behavior when accessing pages marked as read-only. The patch modifies several kernel functions to restore the PAGE_NONE semantics after rewriting a PTE on a VM_UFFD_RWP VMA, thus maintaining the integrity of the RWP marker.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 07/16] mm: handle VM_UFFD_RWP in khugepaged, rmap, and GUP
series_items.patch_summary: This patch extends three mm paths to treat VM_UFFD_RWP (Read/Write Protected) the same as VM_UFFD_WP (Write Protected). The affected paths are khugepaged, rmap, and GUP. In khugepaged, ranges with VM_UFFD_RWP are protected from collapse. In rmap, unmap batching is capped at 1 for VM_UFFD_RWP to ensure each PTE can be handled individually. In GUP, faults are forced through the userfaultfd path even if FOLL_HONOR_NUMA_FAULT is set, as RWP uses protnone as an access-tracking marker rather than a NUMA hinting indicator.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 08/16] userfaultfd: add UFFDIO_REGISTER_MODE_RWP and UFFDIO_RWPROTECT plumbing
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds support for read-write protection tracking to the userfaultfd interface. It introduces two new syscalls, UFFDIO_REGISTER_MODE_RWP and UFFDIO_RWPROTECT, which allow userspace to register a range for RWP tracking and install/remove RWP on that range. The feature is only available on 64-bit kernels with certain architecture features enabled. The patch also includes plumbing for the new syscalls and updates various kernel files to support them.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 09/16] mm/userfaultfd: add RWP fault delivery and expose UFFDIO_REGISTER_MODE_RWP
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds support for read-write protection (RWP) to the userfaultfd mechanism in Linux. It introduces a new RWP-protected PTE type and modifies fault handling code to deliver UFFD_PAGEFAULT_FLAG_RWP messages when an RWP fault occurs. The patch also exposes the UFFDIO_REGISTER_MODE_RWP ioctl, which allows userspace to register for RWP faults. The changes are designed to work with anonymous, shmem, and hugetlb memory types. The patch aims to improve the handling of read-write protection in userfaultfd by providing a more robust and efficient mechanism for delivering RWP faults to userspace.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 10/16] mm/pagemap: add PAGE_IS_ACCESSED for RWP tracking
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new category to the pagemap, called PAGE_IS_ACCESSED, which is used for tracking read-write protection (RWP) in userfaultfd. The existing PAGEMAP_SCAN implementation already reports on write-protected pages using the same bit as RWP, but this is not accurate and can lead to confusion. By introducing a new category specific to RWP, the patch ensures that UAPI does not conflate write protection with read-write protection. Additionally, it updates the pagemap_hugetlb_category function to take into account the VMA's registered mode when reporting on PTE bits.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 11/16] userfaultfd: add UFFD_FEATURE_RWP_ASYNC for async fault resolution
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new feature to the userfaultfd system, called UFFD_FEATURE_RWP_ASYNC. This feature allows for asynchronous resolution of read-write page faults, which is useful for working-set tracking in virtual machines. When this feature is enabled, the kernel restores access to pages in-place and continues without blocking the faulting thread. The VMM can then reconstruct the access pattern after the fact using PAGEMAP_SCAN. This patch also upgrades writable private anon PTEs when necessary to prevent COW-faults on re-access of RWP'd writable pages.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 12/16] userfaultfd: add UFFDIO_SET_MODE for runtime sync/async toggle
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new ioctl called UFFDIO_SET_MODE that allows a VMM to toggle between sync and async read-write-protect (RWP) modes at runtime without re-registering the userfaultfd. The ioctl takes an enable/disable bitmask of UFFD_FEATURE_* flags, with only RWP_ASYNC being toggleable today. When switching to async mode, pending sync waiters are woken so they retry and auto-resolve under the new mode. This allows for dynamic switching between sync and async modes, which is useful for scenarios like working set scanning and page eviction.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 13/16] selftests/mm: add userfaultfd RWP tests
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds userfaultfd (UFFD) read-write-protect (RWP) tests to the Linux kernel selftest suite. The tests cover various scenarios, including async and sync modes, page mapping, mprotect, GUP walks, and fork operations. They verify that RWP works correctly in different memory types, such as anonymous, shared memory, and huge pages. The patch also includes a test for invalid register mode combinations. The goal is to ensure the correctness of UFFD's RWP feature.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 14/16] Documentation/userfaultfd: document RWP working set tracking
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds documentation to the Linux kernel's userfaultfd feature, specifically covering read-write protection (RWP) working set tracking. It explains how RWP works, including its sync and async fault models, UFFDIO_RWPROTECT semantics, and UFFD_FEATURE_RWP_ASYNC. The documentation also outlines a typical workflow for VMM working-set-tracking, from detection to eviction and back to asynchronous mode.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 15/16] userfaultfd.2: Add read-write protect mode
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds support for read-write protect mode to the userfaultfd API, which allows user-space to receive notifications on any access (read or write) to a present page within a registered range. The new registration-mode entry and matching UAPI are documented in the man2/userfaultfd.2 file. The patch also includes documentation for the new pagefault flag and VERSIONS line. This feature is supported starting from Linux 7.2.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 16/16] ioctl_userfaultfd.2: Add read-write protect mode docs
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds documentation to the ioctl_userfaultfd man page for read-write protect mode, which is supported starting from Linux 7.2. The new features include UFFD_FEATURE_RWP and UFFD_FEATURE_RWP_ASYNC capability bits, UFFDIO_REGISTER_MODE_RWP registration-mode bit, and UFFDIO_RWPROTECT and UFFDIO_SET_MODE ioctls for installing and toggling read-write protection. The documentation explains how to use these new features and notes that they require Linux 7.2 or later.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series adds userfaultfd support for tracking the working set of VM guest memory, enabling a VMM to identify cold pages and evict them to tiered or remote storage. The approach uses a new userfaultfd registration mode (MODE_RWP) that marks PTEs as 'userfaultfd-tracked' and allows the kernel to distinguish RWP faults from plain mprotect(PROT_NONE) or NUMA hinting faults.

review_comments.author: Mike Rapoport (Microsoft)
review_comments.summary: Approved the addition of RWP fault delivery and exposed UFFDIO_REGISTER_MODE_RWP, but noted that the feature is not yet advertised and userspace will never see an advertised-but-broken feature.

review_comments.author: Kiryl Shutsemau
review_comments.summary: Responded to Mike Rapoport's approval, noting that the feature is not yet advertised and userspace will never see an advertised-but-broken feature.
review_comments.raw_body:
From: "Kiryl Shutsemau (Meta)" <kas@kernel.org>

pte_protnone() and pmd_protnone() detect present-but-inaccessible page
table entries. This capability is useful beyond NUMA balancing -- for
example, userfaultfd working set tracking uses protnone PTEs to track
page access without unmapping pages.

Introduce CONFIG_ARCH_HAS_PTE_PROTNONE to decouple the protnone PTE
infrastructure from CONFIG_NUMA_BALANCING. The six architectures that
support protnone PTEs (x86_64, arm64, powerpc, s390, riscv, loongarch)
now select this option, and CONFIG_NUMA_BALANCING depends on it.

No functional change -- the same set of architectures continues to have
working protnone support, but the infrastructure is now available
independently of NUMA balancing.

Signed-off-by: Kiryl Shutsemau (Meta) <kas@kernel.org>
Assisted-by: Claude:claude-opus-4-6
Acked-by: SeongJae Park <sj@kernel.org>
Acked-by: Mike Rapoport (Microsoft) <rppt@kernel.org>

diff --git a/include/linux/pgtable.h b/include/linux/pgtable.h

+++ b/include/linux/pgtable.h
@@ -2052,18 +2052,26 @@ static inline int pud_trans_unstable(pud_t *pud)
 	return 0;
 }
 
-#ifndef CONFIG_NUMA_BALANCING
+#ifndef CONFIG_ARCH_HAS_PTE_PROTNONE
 /*
- * In an inaccessible (PROT_NONE) VMA, pte_protnone() may indicate "yes". It is
- * perfectly valid to indicate "no" in that case, which is why our default
- * implementation defaults to "always no".
+ * In an inaccessible (PROT_NONE) VMA, pte_protnone() may indicate "yes". It
+ * is perfectly valid to indicate "no" in that case, which is why our
+ * default implementation defaults to "always no".
  *
- * In an accessible VMA, however, pte_protnone() reliably indicates PROT_NONE
- * page protection due to NUMA hinting. NUMA hinting faults only apply in
- * accessible VMAs.
+ * In an accessible VMA, pte_protnone() reliably indicates a present
+ * PROT_NONE page protection. Today the kernel uses such PTEs for two
+ * purposes: NUMA hinting faults, and userfaultfd RWP tracking on
+ * VM_UFFD_RWP VMAs. The two are distinguished by the uffd PTE bit and
+ * the VMA flag; see include/linux/userfaultfd_k.h.
  *
- * So, to reliably identify PROT_NONE PTEs that require a NUMA hinting fault,
- * looking at the VMA accessibility is sufficient.
+ * So, to reliably identify PROT_NONE PTEs that require kernel handling,
+ * looking at the VMA accessibility (and the uffd bit on RWP VMAs) is
+ * sufficient.
+ *
+ * Architectures without CONFIG_ARCH_HAS_PTE_PROTNONE get the always-zero
+ * stubs below; PAGE_NONE references that survive to runtime fire the
+ * BUILD_BUG() fallback, since callers should have folded such paths to
+ * dead code via IS_ENABLED(CONFIG_ARCH_HAS_PTE_PROTNONE).
  */
 static inline int pte_protnone(pte_t pte)
 {
@@ -2074,7 +2082,11 @@ static inline int pmd_protnone(pmd_t pmd)
 {
 	return 0;
 }
-#endif /* CONFIG_NUMA_BALANCING */
+
+#ifndef PAGE_NONE
+#define PAGE_NONE ({ BUILD_BUG(); (pgprot_t){0}; })
+#endif
+#endif /* CONFIG_ARCH_HAS_PTE_PROTNONE */
 
 #endif /* CONFIG_MMU */
 
diff --git a/init/Kconfig b/init/Kconfig
index 2937c4d308ae..58abb7f19206 100644
--- a/init/Kconfig
+++ b/init/Kconfig
@@ -944,6 +944,13 @@ config SCHED_PROXY_EXEC

+#
+# For architectures that support present-but-inaccessible (PROT_NONE) page
+# table entries detectable via pte_protnone() / pmd_protnone():
+#
+config ARCH_HAS_PTE_PROTNONE
+	bool
+
 #
 # For architectures that want to enable the support for NUMA-affine scheduler
 # balancing logic:
@@ -1010,6 +1017,7 @@ config ARCH_WANT_NUMA_VARIABLE_LOCALITY
 config NUMA_BALANCING
 	bool "Memory placement aware NUMA scheduler"
 	depends on ARCH_SUPPORTS_NUMA_BALANCING
+	depends on ARCH_HAS_PTE_PROTNONE
 	depends on !ARCH_WANT_NUMA_VARIABLE_LOCALITY
 	depends on SMP && NUMA_MIGRATION && !PREEMPT_RT
 	help
diff --git a/mm/debug_vm_pgtable.c b/mm/debug_vm_pgtable.c
index 23dc3ee09561..5e9f3a35f924 100644

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch adds documentation to the Linux kernel's userfaultfd feature, specifically covering read-write protection (RWP) working set tracking. It explains the sync and async fault models, UFFDIO_RWPROTECT semantics, and UFFD_FEATURE_RWP_ASYNC. The documentation also covers a typical workflow for VMM working-set-tracking from detection to eviction and back to async mode.

The tests cover various scenarios, including asynchronous and synchronous RWP modes, page mapping, memory protection, and fork operations. The tests verify that UFFDIO_REGISTER_MODE_RWP and UFFDIO_RWPROTECT work correctly in different situations, ensuring that page permissions are properly restored and protected. The patch includes 11 test cases that cover various types of memory (anon, shmem, hugetlb) and edge cases like fork() and mprotect().

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v2 12/14] userfaultfd: add UFFDIO_SET_MODE for runtime sync/async toggle

version_history.subject: [PATCH v2 12/14] userfaultfd: add UFFDIO_SET_MODE for runtime sync/async toggle

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch adds a new ioctl, UFFDIO_SET_MODE, which allows a VMM to toggle between sync and async RWP modes at runtime without re-registering the userfaultfd. The ioctl takes an enable/disable bitmask of UFFD_FEATURE_* flags, with only UFFD_FEATURE_RWP_ASYNC being toggleable today. When switching to async mode, pending sync waiters are woken so they retry and auto-resolve under the new mode. This patch also includes changes to ensure that readers of ctx->features are properly synchronized with the RMW operation.

version_history.subject: [PATCH v2 11/14] userfaultfd: add UFFD_FEATURE_RWP_ASYNC for async fault resolution

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch adds a new feature to the Linux userfaultfd system, called UFFD_FEATURE_RWP_ASYNC. This feature allows for asynchronous resolution of read-write page faults, which is useful for working-set tracking in virtual machines. The kernel can now restore access to pages in-place without blocking the faulting thread, and the VMM can reconstruct the access pattern after the fact via PAGEMAP_SCAN. Additionally, this patch upgrades writable private anon PTEs when necessary to prevent COW-faults on re-access of RWP'd writable pages.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch adds a new category, PAGE_IS_ACCESSED, to track read-write protection (RWP) in the pagemap. It introduces a new bit in the PTE that is used to indicate whether a page has been accessed or written to, depending on the VMA's registered mode. The patch also updates the UAPI and documentation to reflect this change, ensuring that RWP and write-protected (WP) signals are distinct and scoped to their respective VMAs.

version_history.subject: [PATCH v2 09/14] mm/userfaultfd: add RWP fault delivery and expose UFFDIO_REGISTER_MODE_RWP

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch adds support for read-write protection (RWP) in the Linux kernel's userfaultfd feature. It introduces a new mode, UFFDIO_REGISTER_MODE_RWP, which allows userspace to register RWP-protected pages and receive notifications when access attempts are made on these pages. The patch also updates various fault handling functions to deliver RWP-related messages through the userfaultfd interface. This enables userspace to implement read-write protection policies for specific memory regions. The changes work with anonymous, shared memory, and hugetlb memory types. The patch exposes a new UAPI (UFFD_FEATURE_RWP) that allows userspace to query whether RWP is supported on their system.

version_history.subject: [PATCH v2 05/14] mm: add MM_CP_UFFD_RWP change_protection() flag

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch adds a new change_protection() flag, MM_CP_UFFD_RWP, which is used by userfaultfd (UFFD) to implement read-write protection. The flag installs a PAGE_NONE PTE with the uffd bit set, distinguishing it from other types of faults. This allows UFFD to protect pages from being accessed without its permission. The patch also introduces a new resolve flag, MM_CP_UFFD_RWP_RESOLVE, which restores the original page protection and clears the uffd bit. The changes are applied to various memory management functions, including change_pte_range(), change_huge_pmd(), and hugetlb_change_protection(). This patch is part of a larger series that aims to improve UFFD's functionality and security.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch adds a new feature to the userfaultfd interface, allowing userspace to track read-write protection (RWP) on registered memory ranges. It introduces two new ioctls: UFFDIO_REGISTER_MODE_RWP and UFFDIO_RWPROTECT, which enable RWP tracking and installation/removal of RWP on a range. The patch also includes plumbing for the feature, including capability bits and VMA flags. The goal is to prevent RWP state from outliving the uffd and ensure that re-registering a range does not drop modes that install per-PTE markers.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v2 07/14] mm: handle VM_UFFD_RWP in khugepaged, rmap, and GUP

version_history.subject: [PATCH v2 07/14] mm: handle VM_UFFD_RWP in khugepaged, rmap, and GUP

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series extends three mm paths to handle VM_UFFD_RWP (User Faultfd Read-Write Protected) consistently, ensuring that per-PTE RWP state is not silently destroyed or bypassed. The changes affect khugepaged, rmap, and GUP, with modifications made to their respective code paths to treat VM_UFFD_RWP the same as VM_UFFD_WP.

review_comments.author: Mike Rapoport
review_comments.summary: Acknowledged the patch without raising any concerns or objections.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Fri, May 08, 2026 at 04:55:19PM +0100, Kiryl Shutsemau (Meta) wrote:
> Three mm paths outside the fault handler gate on the uffd PTE bit
> today: khugepaged (skip collapse on ranges carrying markers), rmap
> (cap unmap batching), and GUP (force a fault through
> gup_can_follow_protnone). Extend each to treat VM_UFFD_RWP the same
> as VM_UFFD_WP; otherwise per-PTE RWP state is silently destroyed or
> bypassed.
> 
> khugepaged: try_collapse_pte_mapped_thp() and
> file_backed_vma_is_retractable() already refuse to collapse or
> retract page tables on ranges carrying the uffd PTE bit. Broaden the
> VMA predicate from userfaultfd_wp() to userfaultfd_protected() so
> VM_UFFD_RWP ranges get the same protection. hpage_collapse_scan_pmd()
> needs no change \u2014 its existing pte_uffd() check already catches an
> RWP PTE because it carries the uffd bit.
> 
> rmap: folio_unmap_pte_batch() caps batching at 1 for VM_UFFD_RWP so
> the restore path handles each PTE with its own marker.
> 
> GUP: gup_can_follow_protnone() forces a fault on VM_UFFD_RWP VMAs
> regardless of FOLL_HONOR_NUMA_FAULT. RWP uses protnone as an
> access-tracking marker, not for NUMA hinting, so any GUP \u2014 read or
> write \u2014 must go through the userfaultfd fault path.
> 
> Signed-off-by: Kiryl Shutsemau <kas@kernel.org>
> Assisted-by: Claude:claude-opus-4-6

Acked-by: Mike Rapoport (Microsoft) <rppt@kernel.org>

> ---

>  mm/khugepaged.c    | 18 +++++++++++-------
>  mm/rmap.c          |  2 +-

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch adds a new VMA flag, VM_UFFD_RWP, to enable userfaultfd read-write protection (RWP). The patch includes changes to various files, including mm.h, proc.rst, task_mmu.c, userfaultfd_k.h, trace/events/mmflags.h, and Kconfig.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v2 06/14] mm: preserve RWP marker across PTE rewrites

version_history.subject: [PATCH v2 06/14] mm: preserve RWP marker across PTE rewrites

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch ensures that the RWP (Read-Only Write-Protection) marker is preserved across PTE rewrites in various kernel paths, including swap, device-exclusive, migration, fork, and mprotect operations. Without this preservation, the RWP tracking would be silently dropped, leading to potential security vulnerabilities. The patch updates several kernel functions to maintain the RWP bit when rewriting PTEs on VM_UFFD_RWP VMA pages, ensuring that the marker is correctly propagated across different memory management scenarios.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch decouples the protnone helpers from CONFIG_NUMA_BALANCING, making them available independently of NUMA balancing.
